---
title: "Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"    # data points / vectors
ARROW  = "#c1121f"    # the highlighted arrow / dependent direction
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / independent directions
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fourth article in the **Geometry of
> Data** track and the capstone of its first movement. It assumes
> [GEOM-0 *What Is a Vector, Really?*] (a vector is a point, an arrow, and a row of data),
> [GEOM-1 *Adding and Scaling*] (the linear combination $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k$
> is the one move from which everything is built), and [GEOM-2 *Span and Subspaces*] (the
> set of all linear combinations of some vectors is the subspace they reach). Here we ask
> the question those three articles set up but did not answer: *which of the spanning
> vectors are actually pulling weight, and how few do we really need?* It sets up
> [GEOM-5 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*] (the nicest bases), [GEOM-12 *Rank and
> the Four Fundamental Subspaces*] (rank is the dimension of the column space), and
> [GEOM-14 *Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors*] (an eigenbasis is a basis chosen to make a map
> transparent).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

You have a handful of vectors and they span some subspace — a line, a plane, a flat in
$\mathbb R^n$. Two questions remain, and they are the same question asked twice. *Is any
one of these vectors redundant — already reachable from the others?* And *what is the
smallest set that still reaches every point of the subspace?* The first question is
**linear independence**; the second is **basis**; and the punchline that ties them
together is **dimension** — the number of vectors in a basis, which we prove is the same
no matter which basis you pick. This article defines all three from the single equation
$c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0$, reads each geometrically (no vector
lies in the span of the others), proves the one theorem that makes "dimension" a
well-defined number at all, and shows what coordinates *mean* once a basis is fixed. The
single claim it defends: **a subspace has an intrinsic size, independent of how you
describe it, and a basis is the most efficient possible description — exactly as many
directions as the space has, with none wasted.** We close on the data reading that the
rest of the series needs: a redundant feature is a linearly dependent column, and the
number of features you actually have is the dimension of the space they span, not the
count of columns in your table.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A set of vectors is **linearly independent** when the *only* way to combine them into the
zero vector is to multiply every one of them by zero — no vector is secretly built from
the others. A **basis** for a subspace is a linearly independent set that also spans it:
**exactly enough directions to reach everything, and not one to spare.** Every basis of a
given subspace has the *same number* of vectors, and that number is the subspace's
**dimension** — its intrinsic size, the count of independent directions you can move in
without leaving it.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The two ideas — "spans the space" and "wastes nothing"
— pull in opposite directions. Spanning wants *more* vectors (so you can reach more);
independence wants *fewer* (so nothing is redundant). A basis is precisely where those two
pressures balance, and dimension is the count at the balance point. The deepest claim, and
the one that takes a real proof, is that this count is forced: you cannot describe a plane
with a basis of two vectors and also with a basis of three. The plane *is* two-dimensional,
and no honest description can pretend otherwise.

# Why we are doing this: the redundancy problem {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when does a collection of vectors carry
redundant information, and what is the leanest collection that loses nothing?** Every
definition and every proof below answers a piece of that.

## What GEOM-2 left open

[GEOM-2] handed us the *span*: given vectors $\mathbf v_1, \dots, \mathbf v_k$, the set of
all linear combinations $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k$ is a subspace — a line, a
plane, or some flat through the origin. But it also showed, in its closing edge case,
something unsettling: **the number of vectors you start with need not match the dimension
of what they reach.** Three vectors can span a mere plane if one of them lies flat in the
plane of the other two. Two vectors can span only a line if they point along the same
direction. The count of vectors is a property of *your description*; the size of the
subspace is a property of *the subspace*. They are not the same, and conflating them is
the source of a great deal of confusion in data analysis.

This gap is not academic. It is the abstract skeleton of three concrete situations a data
scientist meets constantly:

- **A redundant feature.** You record a person's height in centimeters *and* in inches.
  The second column is $2.54$ times the first — it adds a vector to your table but not a
  direction to your data. Any model that tries to use both will choke (this is exact
  multicollinearity, the failure mode [REGR-7] is built around).
- **A constraint you did not notice.** Your features are the fractions of a budget spent on
  food, rent, and "everything else." They sum to $1$ by construction, so the third is
  $1$ minus the first two: one fewer independent direction than you have columns.
- **The intrinsic dimension of data.** A dataset of $1000$ features may, because the
  features are correlated, really live near a $20$-dimensional flat. Discovering that
  number — the *true* count of independent directions — is the whole point of dimension
  reduction (PCA, owned by Unsupervised Learning, built on the SVD of [GEOM-20]).

In every case the question is the same: **how many of these directions are real, and which
are echoes of the others?** Linear independence is the precise tool for answering it.

## The history: how "dimension" became a number

The intuition that a line is one-dimensional, a plane two, and space three is older than
algebra — it is in Euclid, as the idea that a solid is bounded by surfaces, a surface by
lines, a line by points. But that is a *picture*, not a definition, and it stops working
the moment you cannot draw the space. What is the dimension of the set of solutions to
three equations in seven unknowns? You cannot eyeball it.

The arithmetic notion we use was forged in the nineteenth century. Hermann Grassmann, in
his nearly unreadable 1844 *Ausdehnungslehre* ("Theory of Extension"), was the first to
treat dimension as the number of independent quantities needed to specify a point, freed
entirely from three-dimensional space. Around the same time the study of systems of linear
equations — by Gauss, then sharpened by Cayley and Sylvester, who coined "matrix" in the
1850s — made "how many equations are genuinely independent" a computational question. The
synthesis, that the *count of independent directions is the same for every basis and
therefore a property of the space itself*, is the theorem we prove in @sec-invariance. It
is what licenses the single word "dimension." Without it, "dimension" would be an artifact
of how you happened to write the space down — and the entire geometric program of this
track, *where is this in space and what is it doing*, would have no firm ground to stand
on.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why isn't 'dimension' just the number of vectors you were handed?"* Because that number
is up to you — you can always hand over more vectors than you need, including redundant
ones, and you can sometimes get away with fewer than you expected if some are wasted.
Dimension has to be a property of the *subspace*, not of any particular list describing it.
Making that precise — proving the count cannot depend on the list — is exactly what
@thm-invariance does. An interviewer asking this is checking whether you know that
"dimension" is an earned word, not a free one.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture three vectors in $\mathbb R^3$. Two cases.

**Case A — they are independent.** $\mathbf v_1$ points along one direction, $\mathbf v_2$
along a genuinely different one (not a stretch of $\mathbf v_1$), and $\mathbf v_3$ pokes
*out of the plane* the first two define. To reach $\mathbf v_3$ you cannot stay inside that
plane, so $\mathbf v_3$ is not built from $\mathbf v_1$ and $\mathbf v_2$. Each vector adds
a direction the others could not reach. Together they span all of $\mathbb R^3$, and you
need every one of them.

**Case B — one is redundant.** Same $\mathbf v_1$ and $\mathbf v_2$, but now $\mathbf v_3$
*lies flat in their plane* — it is some combination $\mathbf v_3 = 2\mathbf v_1 - \mathbf
v_2$, say. It adds nothing: anything you could reach with all three, you could already
reach with the first two. The three vectors span only a plane, not all of space. One
direction is an echo.

@fig-independence draws exactly these two cases. The whole of linear independence is the
algebraic test that distinguishes them — that catches "$\mathbf v_3$ lies in the span of
the others" without your having to *see* the picture, which matters enormously the moment
the space has more than three dimensions and cannot be drawn at all.

```{python}
#| label: fig-independence
#| fig-cap: "The geometric heart of independence in $\\mathbb R^3$. **Left (independent):** $\\mathbf v_3$ (red) lifts out of the plane spanned by $\\mathbf v_1,\\mathbf v_2$ (green), so it cannot be built from them — three real directions, spanning all of space. **Right (dependent):** $\\mathbf v_3$ lies *in* that plane ($\\mathbf v_3 = 2\\mathbf v_1 - \\mathbf v_2$), an echo of the others — only two real directions, spanning a mere plane. Linear independence is the algebra that tells these apart without needing to see them."

def draw_vec(ax, v, color, label, lw=2.5):
    ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, v[0], v[1], v[2], color=color, lw=lw,
              arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(v[0]*1.08, v[1]*1.08, v[2]*1.08, label, color=color, fontsize=11)

v1 = np.array([1.6, 0.2, 0.0])
v2 = np.array([0.3, 1.6, 0.0])

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8.6, 4.2))

# Left: independent
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
# faint plane of v1,v2
ss, tt = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-0.2, 1.1, 6), np.linspace(-0.2, 1.1, 6))
PX = ss*v1[0] + tt*v2[0]; PY = ss*v1[1] + tt*v2[1]; PZ = ss*v1[2] + tt*v2[2]
axL.plot_surface(PX, PY, PZ, color=GREY, alpha=0.18, linewidth=0)
v3a = np.array([0.4, 0.4, 1.6])
draw_vec(axL, v1, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf v_1$")
draw_vec(axL, v2, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf v_2$")
draw_vec(axL, v3a, ARROW, r"$\mathbf v_3$")
axL.set_title("Independent: $\\mathbf v_3$ lifts off the plane")
axL.set_xlim(0, 1.8); axL.set_ylim(0, 1.8); axL.set_zlim(0, 1.8)
axL.set_xticks([]); axL.set_yticks([]); axL.set_zticks([])
axL.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)

# Right: dependent
axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2, projection="3d")
axR.plot_surface(PX, PY, PZ, color=GREY, alpha=0.18, linewidth=0)
v3b = 2*v1 - v2   # lies in the plane
draw_vec(axR, v1, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf v_1$")
draw_vec(axR, v2, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf v_2$")
draw_vec(axR, v3b, ARROW, r"$\mathbf v_3$")
axR.set_title("Dependent: $\\mathbf v_3$ lies in the plane")
axR.set_xlim(0, 3.4); axR.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.8); axR.set_zlim(0, 1.8)
axR.set_xticks([]); axR.set_yticks([]); axR.set_zticks([])
axR.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The phrase to hold onto is *"no vector lies in the span of the others."* That is what
independence means in pictures. The formal definition below looks different — it talks
about combinations equal to zero — and the first real task is to prove the picture and the
formula are the same statement.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Linear independence, defined {#sec-li-def}

We work inside $\mathbb R^n$ throughout, with vectors written in bold and scalars (ordinary
real numbers) in plain type. Recall from [GEOM-1] that a **linear combination** of
$\mathbf v_1, \dots, \mathbf v_k$ is any vector of the form $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots +
c_k\mathbf v_k$, where the $c_i$ are scalars called the **coefficients** or **weights**.
Recall too the **zero vector** $\mathbf 0 = (0, 0, \dots, 0)$, the origin, which every
subspace contains.

There is always *one* trivial way to combine any vectors into $\mathbf 0$: take every
coefficient to be zero. We call $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0$ with
$c_1 = c_2 = \dots = c_k = 0$ the **trivial combination**. It is uninteresting precisely
because it always works, for any vectors whatsoever — it says nothing about *them*. The
entire content of independence is whether there is *any other* way.

::: {#def-independence}
**Definition (linear independence).** The vectors $\mathbf v_1, \dots, \mathbf v_k$ are
**linearly independent** if the equation

$$
c_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\mathbf v_2 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0
$$ {#eq-li}

has *only* the trivial solution $c_1 = c_2 = \dots = c_k = 0$. If @eq-li has any solution
in which at least one coefficient is nonzero, the vectors are **linearly dependent**, and
such a nonzero solution is called a **dependence relation** among them.
:::

Read @eq-li as a question, not a statement: *across all choices of coefficients, is zero
the only combination that lands on the origin?* "Yes, only the trivial one" is independence.
"No, here is a combination with a nonzero coefficient that also lands on zero" is
dependence, and that very combination is the evidence.

::: {.callout-important title="The pivot: the coefficients are the unknowns"}
As in the least-squares pivot of [REGR-1], notice *what is variable here*. The vectors
$\mathbf v_1, \dots, \mathbf v_k$ are fixed, given. The unknowns we solve @eq-li *for* are
the coefficients $c_1, \dots, c_k$. Independence is a statement about the **solution set
of this homogeneous equation** — does it contain only the origin of coefficient-space, or
more? Every test in this article reduces to solving @eq-li for the $c_i$.
:::

## The definition *is* the geometric picture {#sec-li-geometry}

The definition mentions only "combinations equal to zero." The intuition of @sec-intuition
spoke of "no vector lies in the span of the others." We now prove these are the same
statement — no hand-waving, both directions.

::: {#lem-li-equiv}
**Lemma (the two faces of dependence).** For vectors $\mathbf v_1, \dots, \mathbf v_k$ with
$k \ge 2$, the following are equivalent:

1. The vectors are linearly *dependent* (there is a dependence relation, @eq-li with some
   coefficient nonzero).
2. At least one of the vectors can be written as a linear combination of the others — it
   lies in their span.
:::

::: {.proof}
We show each statement forces the other.

**(1) $\Rightarrow$ (2).** Suppose there is a dependence relation
$c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0$ in which some coefficient is nonzero;
say $c_j \neq 0$ for a particular index $j$. Isolate the $j$-th term by subtracting every
other term from both sides:

$$
c_j\mathbf v_j = -\sum_{i \neq j} c_i\mathbf v_i .
$$

Because $c_j \neq 0$ we may divide both sides by it (a nonzero scalar has a reciprocal),
giving

$$
\mathbf v_j = \sum_{i \neq j} \left(-\frac{c_i}{c_j}\right)\mathbf v_i .
$$ {#eq-vj-from-others}

The right-hand side is a linear combination of the *other* vectors, so $\mathbf v_j$ lies
in their span. That is statement (2).

**(2) $\Rightarrow$ (1).** Conversely, suppose some $\mathbf v_j$ is a combination of the
others, $\mathbf v_j = \sum_{i \neq j} a_i\mathbf v_i$ for some scalars $a_i$. Move
everything to one side by subtracting $\mathbf v_j$:

$$
\Bigl(\sum_{i \neq j} a_i\mathbf v_i\Bigr) - \mathbf v_j = \mathbf 0 .
$$

This is a combination of *all* the vectors equal to $\mathbf 0$, and the coefficient on
$\mathbf v_j$ is $-1$, which is nonzero. So it is a dependence relation — statement (1).
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So @def-independence and the picture agree exactly: **dependent** means "one of them is a
redundant echo of the others," and the dependence relation @eq-li is just that echo with
everything moved to one side. @eq-vj-from-others is worth remembering — it is the recipe
for reading *which* vector is redundant and *how* it is built, straight off the
coefficients.

One special case deserves a sentence, because it trips people up. A single nonzero vector
$\mathbf v_1$ is always independent: $c_1\mathbf v_1 = \mathbf 0$ with $\mathbf v_1 \neq
\mathbf 0$ forces $c_1 = 0$. But any set that *contains the zero vector* is automatically
dependent — put coefficient $1$ on the zero vector and $0$ on everything else, and you get
$\mathbf 0$ with a nonzero coefficient. The origin is the ultimate redundant direction: it
points nowhere.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me the fastest check for whether two vectors are independent."* Two vectors are
dependent if and only if one is a scalar multiple of the other — i.e. they are parallel
(or antiparallel), lying on the same line through the origin. For exactly two vectors,
@lem-li-equiv collapses to this: "$\mathbf v_2$ lies in the span of $\mathbf v_1$" just
means $\mathbf v_2 = a\mathbf v_1$. So for *two* vectors, independence = "not parallel."
For three or more there is no such shortcut — one vector can be a combination of two others
without being a multiple of either — and you must actually solve @eq-li.
:::

## How you actually test it: solving the homogeneous system {#sec-li-test}

Independence is defined by @eq-li, so testing it means solving @eq-li. Writing it out
coordinate by coordinate turns it into a system of linear equations. Suppose each
$\mathbf v_i \in \mathbb R^n$. The vector equation $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k
= \mathbf 0$ is *one equation per coordinate* — $n$ scalar equations in the $k$ unknowns
$c_1, \dots, c_k$. Stacking the vectors as the columns of a matrix $A = [\,\mathbf v_1 \mid
\mathbf v_2 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf v_k\,]$ and the coefficients into a column $\mathbf c =
(c_1, \dots, c_k)$, the whole thing is

$$
A\mathbf c = \mathbf 0 .
$$ {#eq-Ac-zero}

(That $A\mathbf c$ equals the combination $c_1\mathbf v_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf v_k$ — a
matrix times a vector is a weighted sum of the matrix's columns — is the central reading of
matrix–vector multiplication, built in full in [GEOM-8]. We use only that fact here; the
rest of matrix algebra is not needed yet.) So:

> **The vectors are independent if and only if $A\mathbf c = \mathbf 0$ has only the
> solution $\mathbf c = \mathbf 0$** — the homogeneous system has no nontrivial solution.

We solve such a system by **Gaussian elimination**: row-reduce $A$ to a staircase
(row-echelon) form and read off the solutions. The mechanics of elimination get their
proper treatment in [GEOM-11]; here we need only its verdict, which is clean. After
row-reducing, each column either has a leading entry of its staircase (a **pivot**) or it
does not (a **free column**). A free column means its coefficient $c_i$ can be set to
anything — a nontrivial solution exists — so:

$$
\text{independent} \iff \text{every column is a pivot column} \iff \text{no free variables.}
$$

This already yields a result you can use without any computation at all.

::: {#thm-too-many}
**Theorem (too many vectors must be dependent).** Any set of $k$ vectors in $\mathbb R^n$
with $k > n$ is linearly dependent.
:::

::: {.proof}
The matrix $A = [\,\mathbf v_1 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf v_k\,]$ has $n$ rows and $k$ columns.
Row-reduction produces at most one pivot per row, so there are at most $n$ pivot columns.
But there are $k > n$ columns in total, so at least $k - n \ge 1$ columns have no pivot —
they are free columns. A free column gives a free variable, hence a nontrivial solution to
$A\mathbf c = \mathbf 0$, hence a dependence relation. So the vectors are dependent.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@thm-too-many is the first concrete payoff and it is purely about counting: **you cannot
have more than $n$ independent directions in $\mathbb R^n$.** Five vectors in the plane
$\mathbb R^2$? Dependent, guaranteed, before you look at a single number. This is the seed
of the dimension bound we make exact in @sec-invariance.

## Basis: spanning *and* independent {#sec-basis}

We now have the two pressures from the Core, made precise. *Spanning* (from [GEOM-2]) is
the demand that the vectors reach every point of the subspace: their span is all of it.
*Independence* (@def-independence) is the demand that none is wasted. A basis is the set
that does both.

::: {#def-basis}
**Definition (basis).** A set of vectors $\mathbf b_1, \dots, \mathbf b_m$ in a subspace
$V$ is a **basis** for $V$ if

1. they **span** $V$ — every vector in $V$ is some linear combination of them — and
2. they are **linearly independent** — no dependence relation @eq-li among them except the
   trivial one.
:::

A basis is therefore a spanning set with all the fat trimmed off, or equivalently a maximal
independent set that still reaches everything. The two conditions are exactly the two
opposing pressures meeting in the middle: condition 1 forbids "too few," condition 2 forbids
"too many."

The cleanest example is the **standard basis** of $\mathbb R^n$. Let $\mathbf e_i$ be the
vector with a $1$ in position $i$ and $0$ everywhere else: $\mathbf e_1 = (1, 0, \dots, 0)$,
$\mathbf e_2 = (0, 1, 0, \dots, 0)$, and so on. They span $\mathbb R^n$ because any
$\mathbf x = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ is visibly $x_1\mathbf e_1 + \dots + x_n\mathbf e_n$ — the
coordinates *are* the coefficients. And they are independent because $c_1\mathbf e_1 + \dots
+ c_n\mathbf e_n = (c_1, \dots, c_n)$, which equals $\mathbf 0$ only when every $c_i = 0$.
So $\{\mathbf e_1, \dots, \mathbf e_n\}$ is a basis of $\mathbb R^n$, the one you have been
using implicitly since you first wrote a vector as a list of numbers.

But it is *a* basis, not *the* basis — and this is the conceptual leap of the section.

::: {#thm-unique-coords}
**Theorem (unique coordinates).** If $\mathbf b_1, \dots, \mathbf b_m$ is a basis for $V$,
then every $\mathbf x \in V$ has **exactly one** representation as a combination of the
basis vectors:

$$
\mathbf x = c_1\mathbf b_1 + c_2\mathbf b_2 + \dots + c_m\mathbf b_m
$$ {#eq-coords}

for a unique list of scalars $(c_1, \dots, c_m)$, called the **coordinates of $\mathbf x$
relative to the basis**.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Existence* of some representation is immediate: the basis spans $V$, so by condition 1 of
@def-basis any $\mathbf x \in V$ is *some* combination of the $\mathbf b_i$.

*Uniqueness* is where independence earns its keep. Suppose $\mathbf x$ had two
representations,

$$
\mathbf x = c_1\mathbf b_1 + \dots + c_m\mathbf b_m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\mathbf x = d_1\mathbf b_1 + \dots + d_m\mathbf b_m .
$$

Subtract the second from the first. The left side is $\mathbf x - \mathbf x = \mathbf 0$,
and grouping the right side term by term,

$$
\mathbf 0 = (c_1 - d_1)\mathbf b_1 + (c_2 - d_2)\mathbf b_2 + \dots + (c_m - d_m)\mathbf b_m .
$$

This is a combination of the basis vectors equal to $\mathbf 0$. But the $\mathbf b_i$ are
independent (condition 2 of @def-basis), so by @def-independence *every* coefficient must be
zero: $c_i - d_i = 0$ for each $i$, i.e. $c_i = d_i$ for all $i$. The two representations
were identical all along. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is *why* independence is in the definition of a basis at all. A merely-spanning set
gives every vector *at least* one coordinate representation; adding independence makes it
*exactly* one. Coordinates are only well-defined — only a genuine address for each point —
when the describing set is independent. With a redundant vector around, the same point would
have infinitely many "addresses," and "the coordinates of $\mathbf x$" would be meaningless.
A basis is precisely the data structure that gives every point of a subspace one
unambiguous address.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does it mean to 'change basis,' and why would you ever want to?"* @thm-unique-coords
says coordinates are relative to a chosen basis — the *same* geometric point gets *different*
coordinate lists in different bases. Changing basis means re-expressing every point's
address in a new, more convenient set of directions while leaving the points themselves
untouched. You want to when some basis makes the structure obvious: the eigenbasis of
[GEOM-14] turns a complicated map into independent stretches along its own axes; the
principal-component basis (Unsupervised Learning, on the SVD of [GEOM-20]) lines the axes
up with the directions of greatest variance so the first few coordinates capture most of
the data. The geometry never changes; only the description does.
:::

## Dimension: proving the count is forced {#sec-invariance}

We can now reach the article's hardest and most important result. A subspace can have many
different bases — the standard basis of $\mathbb R^2$ is $\{(1,0), (0,1)\}$, but $\{(1,1),
(1,-1)\}$ is *also* a basis (independent, since neither is a multiple of the other, and
spanning, as a short check confirms). They look nothing alike. Yet both have **two**
vectors. Is that a coincidence, or a law?

It is a law, and proving it is what makes "dimension" a legitimate word. The engine is the
following lemma, the single most important counting fact in linear algebra.

::: {#lem-steinitz}
**Lemma (independent $\le$ spanning).** Suppose the vectors $\mathbf w_1, \dots, \mathbf
w_p$ all lie in the span of $\mathbf u_1, \dots, \mathbf u_q$. If the $\mathbf w$'s are
linearly independent, then $p \le q$. In words: **an independent set can never be larger
than a spanning set of the same space.**
:::

::: {.proof}
Each $\mathbf w_j$ lies in the span of the $\mathbf u_i$, so we can write every $\mathbf
w_j$ as a combination of the $\mathbf u$'s, with some coefficients $a_{ij}$:

$$
\mathbf w_j = a_{1j}\mathbf u_1 + a_{2j}\mathbf u_2 + \dots + a_{qj}\mathbf u_q,
\qquad j = 1, \dots, p .
$$ {#eq-w-in-u}

We will *suppose for contradiction that $p > q$* and derive a dependence relation among the
$\mathbf w$'s, contradicting their independence.

Consider an unknown combination of the $\mathbf w$'s set to zero,

$$
x_1\mathbf w_1 + x_2\mathbf w_2 + \dots + x_p\mathbf w_p = \mathbf 0 ,
$$ {#eq-x-combo}

and ask whether the unknowns $x_1, \dots, x_p$ can be anything other than all zero.
Substitute each $\mathbf w_j$ from @eq-w-in-u into @eq-x-combo:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{p} x_j \Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{q} a_{ij}\mathbf u_i\Bigr) = \mathbf 0 .
$$

Swap the order of the two sums (a finite sum may be reordered freely) so as to collect the
coefficient of each $\mathbf u_i$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{q} \Bigl(\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{ij} x_j}_{\text{coefficient of } \mathbf u_i}\Bigr)\mathbf u_i = \mathbf 0 .
$$

This certainly holds if every one of those inner coefficients is zero. That is a system of
$q$ equations — one for each $i$ — in the $p$ unknowns $x_1, \dots, x_p$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{ij} x_j = 0, \qquad i = 1, \dots, q .
$$ {#eq-homog-sys}

Now count. @eq-homog-sys is a homogeneous linear system with $q$ equations and $p$
unknowns, and we assumed $p > q$ — *more unknowns than equations.* A homogeneous system
with more unknowns than equations always has a nontrivial solution: row-reduction yields at
most $q$ pivots among the $p$ columns, leaving at least $p - q \ge 1$ free variables, each
of which can be set nonzero (this is the same pivot-counting argument as in @thm-too-many).
So there exist values $x_1, \dots, x_p$, *not all zero*, satisfying @eq-homog-sys — and
hence, walking back up the chain, satisfying @eq-x-combo.

But @eq-x-combo with not-all-zero $x_j$ is a *nontrivial* dependence relation among the
$\mathbf w$'s. That contradicts the assumption that the $\mathbf w$'s are independent. The
contradiction came solely from supposing $p > q$, so that supposition is false: $p \le q$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This lemma is the whole ballgame. Read it as a fundamental tension made quantitative: *you
cannot fit more independent directions into a space than it takes vectors to span it.*
Independence pushes the count down toward the true size; spanning pushes it up toward the
true size; the lemma says independent never exceeds spanning. Apply it twice and the two
pressures pin the number exactly.

::: {#thm-invariance}
**Theorem (invariance of dimension).** Any two bases of the same subspace $V$ have the same
number of vectors.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $\mathbf b_1, \dots, \mathbf b_p$ and $\mathbf b'_1, \dots, \mathbf b'_q$ both be bases
of $V$. Each is, by @def-basis, simultaneously independent *and* spanning.

Apply @lem-steinitz once: the first basis is independent, and the second basis spans $V$
(so the first set lies in the second's span). Therefore $p \le q$.

Apply @lem-steinitz again with the roles swapped: the second basis is independent, and the
first basis spans $V$. Therefore $q \le p$.

From $p \le q$ and $q \le p$ we conclude $p = q$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Now — and only now — the word is earned.

::: {#def-dimension}
**Definition (dimension).** The **dimension** of a subspace $V$, written $\dim V$, is the
number of vectors in any basis of $V$. By @thm-invariance this number does not depend on
which basis is chosen, so it is a genuine property of $V$ itself. The dimension of the zero
subspace $\{\mathbf 0\}$ is defined to be $0$ (its only "basis" is the empty set; it has no
directions to move in).
:::

So a line through the origin has dimension $1$, a plane through the origin dimension $2$,
all of $\mathbb R^n$ dimension $n$ (its standard basis has $n$ vectors), and the bound of
@thm-too-many becomes the crisp statement $\dim V \le n$ for every subspace $V$ of $\mathbb
R^n$. Dimension is the *intrinsic count of independent directions* — what Euclid pictured
and Grassmann made arithmetic.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If I have a spanning set that is too big, or an independent set that is too small, can I
always fix it into a basis?"* Yes, both directions, and it is worth knowing why.
**Trimming:** if a spanning set is dependent, @lem-li-equiv finds a vector that is a
combination of the others; deleting it shrinks the set but not the span (you could already
reach the deleted vector), so you can keep deleting until what remains is independent — a
basis. **Extending:** if an independent set does not yet span $V$, there is a vector in $V$
outside their span; adjoining it keeps the set independent (it cannot be a combination of
the others, since it is outside their span — the contrapositive of @lem-li-equiv) and grows
the span, so you can keep adjoining until you reach all of $V$. Both processes halt, because
@lem-steinitz caps the size at $\dim V$. Every spanning set contains a basis; every
independent set extends to one.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run these ideas on real, messy data — never a toy rigged to flatter the technique.
The first example we carry through by hand so you watch the test breathe; the second pushes
it to the honest edge.

## Example 1 — a redundant feature in a data table {#sec-ex-redundant}

This is the canonical data-science encounter with dependence. An analyst assembles a small
feature table for a set of properties: the **floor area** in square meters, the same area
in **square feet**, and the **number of rooms**. Treating each *feature* (column) as a
vector in $\mathbb R^n$ — one entry per property — we ask: how many independent feature
directions are really here?

```{python}
#| label: tbl-redundant
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

# Six properties; three recorded features (columns).
area_m2   = np.array([ 50.,  72.,  95., 110., 130., 60.])
area_sqft = area_m2 * 10.7639          # the SAME area, different unit
rooms     = np.array([  2.,   3.,   4.,   4.,   5.,  3.])

A = np.column_stack([area_m2, area_sqft, rooms])   # columns = feature vectors
print("feature matrix A (rows = properties, cols = features):")
print(A)

# Numerical rank = number of independent columns = dimension of their span.
print("\nnumber of features (columns) :", A.shape[1])
print("rank (independent directions):", np.linalg.matrix_rank(A))
```

The table has **three** feature columns, but the rank — the dimension of the space those
columns span, which is exactly the maximum number of independent ones — comes back as
**two**. One feature is an echo. We can see precisely which, and prove the dependence by
hand. Square feet is square meters times the fixed conversion factor $10.7639$, so as
*vectors*,

$$
\mathbf{a}_{\text{sqft}} = 10.7639\,\mathbf{a}_{\text{m}^2} .
$$

Rearranged into the dependence-relation form of @eq-li, with the room vector $\mathbf
r$ getting coefficient zero,

$$
10.7639\,\mathbf{a}_{\text{m}^2} \;-\; 1\cdot\mathbf{a}_{\text{sqft}} \;+\; 0\cdot\mathbf r = \mathbf 0 ,
$$

a combination of the three feature vectors equal to $\mathbf 0$ with a *nonzero*
coefficient ($-1$ on square feet). By @def-independence the three columns are linearly
dependent, and by @lem-li-equiv one of them — the square-feet column — lies in the span of
the others. It is a direction the data already had. @fig-redundant draws the three feature
vectors restricted to two of the properties, so the collinearity of the two area columns is
visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-redundant
#| fig-cap: "Why the rank is 2, not 3. Plotting each feature column using just two of the properties as coordinates, the two area features (square meters, green; square feet, red) lie on the *same line through the origin* — square feet is exactly $10.7639\\times$ square meters, so it adds no new direction. The room feature (blue) points off that line, a genuine second direction. Three columns, two independent directions: $\\dim(\\text{span}) = 2$."
# Use properties 0 and 4 as the two coordinate axes for a 2D picture.
i, j = 0, 4
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.2))

def arrow(ax, v, color, label):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(v[0], v[1]), xytext=(0, 0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=2.4))
    ax.text(v[0]*1.04, v[1]*1.04, label, color=color, fontsize=11)

am = np.array([area_m2[i],   area_m2[j]])
af = np.array([area_sqft[i], area_sqft[j]]) / 10.7639   # rescaled to fit the frame
ar = np.array([rooms[i],     rooms[j]])

arrow(ax, am, ACCENT, r"area (m$^2$)")
arrow(ax, af*0.78, ARROW, r"area (sqft, rescaled)")   # same direction as m^2
arrow(ax, ar*22,  POINT, "rooms")
# show the shared line through origin for the two area features
t = np.linspace(-0.2, 1.25, 50)
ax.plot(t*am[0], t*am[1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0, zorder=0,
        label="shared direction of both area features")
ax.set_xlim(-10, 140); ax.set_ylim(-10, 140)
ax.set_xlabel(f"coordinate from property {i+1}")
ax.set_ylabel(f"coordinate from property {j+1}")
ax.set_title("Two area features share one direction")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The practitioner lesson, which the rest of the series leans on hard: **the number of
columns in your table is not the dimensionality of your data.** Here you have three columns
but two directions. Feeding both area columns into a linear model is exact
multicollinearity — the design matrix $X^\top X$ becomes singular and the fit has no unique
solution, the disaster [REGR-7] is built around. The cure is to recognize the dependence
and drop the redundant column, restoring an independent — hence basis-like — set of
features.

## Example 2 — the budget-share constraint (a subtler dependence) {#sec-ex-budget}

The first example was a redundancy you could spot by eye. The second is the kind you cannot,
because no single column is a multiple of another — the dependence is spread across three
of them. Households report the fraction of spending going to **food**, **housing**, and
**other**. These are shares, so by construction they sum to $1$ for every household. We add
a constant **intercept** feature (a column of ones), as every regression silently does.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-budget
#| echo: true
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 8
food    = rng.uniform(0.20, 0.45, n)
housing = rng.uniform(0.25, 0.50, n)
other   = 1.0 - food - housing          # forced: the three shares sum to 1
intercept = np.ones(n)                   # the constant feature regressions add

B = np.column_stack([intercept, food, housing, other])
print("columns: [intercept, food, housing, other]")
print(np.round(B, 3))
print("\nnumber of features (columns) :", B.shape[1])
print("rank (independent directions):", np.linalg.matrix_rank(B))
```

Four columns, rank **three**. No column is a multiple of any other — food is not a scaled
copy of housing — so the pairwise eyeball test of @sec-li-geometry is useless here. Yet a
dependence relation exists, and we can write it down exactly. Since for every household
$\text{food} + \text{housing} + \text{other} = 1$, and the intercept column is all ones,

$$
1\cdot\mathbf{intercept} \;-\; 1\cdot\mathbf{food} \;-\; 1\cdot\mathbf{housing} \;-\; 1\cdot\mathbf{other} = \mathbf 0 .
$$

Every coefficient is $\pm 1$ — all nonzero where it matters — so this is a genuine
dependence relation among the four columns. The four features span only a *three*-dimensional
space; one degree of freedom was spent the moment the shares were forced to sum to one. This
is the famous **dummy-variable trap** in disguise: include a full set of mutually exclusive
shares (or category indicators) *and* an intercept, and you have built an invisible
dependence into your design. The fix is to drop one — any one — restoring independence, with
the dropped category absorbed into the intercept as a baseline.

The honest moral spanning both examples: **dependence is a property you must test for, not
see.** In two columns you can spot it (parallel); in three or more, as @lem-li-equiv warned,
a vector can be a combination of others without being a multiple of any single one, and only
solving @eq-li — i.e. computing the rank — reveals it.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Linear independence is a *clean binary in exact arithmetic* — vectors are independent or
they are not, full stop. The trouble is that real data and real computers do not live in
exact arithmetic, and the binary frays at the edges. This section is the honest boundary.

## Near-dependence: the binary becomes a dial {#sec-near-dependence}

The definition asks whether @eq-li has *any* nonzero solution. But what if the only nonzero
solutions are tiny — if the vectors are not parallel but *nearly* so? Mathematically they
are independent; practically they may as well not be. @fig-near draws two vectors swinging
from clearly independent toward nearly collinear, and tracks the smallest singular value of
the matrix they form — the standard quantitative measure of "how close to dependent."

```{python}
#| label: fig-near
#| fig-cap: "Independence is exact; near-dependence is a dial. As the angle between two unit vectors shrinks from $90^\\circ$ toward $0^\\circ$ (left to right, top), they remain technically independent until the instant they coincide — but the smallest singular value $\\sigma_{\\min}$ of the matrix $[\\mathbf v_1\\mid\\mathbf v_2]$ (bottom) slides toward zero, the continuous measure of how nearly dependent they are. At tiny angles the pair is independent in theory and useless in practice: this is the geometry of multicollinearity and ill-conditioning."
angles = np.array([90, 60, 30, 12, 4])   # degrees between the two vectors
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.2, 6.0),
                         gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": [1.1, 1]})

# Top: the vector pairs at a few angles
axT = axes[0]
offsets = np.linspace(0, 9, len(angles))
for k, (deg, off) in enumerate(zip(angles, offsets)):
    th = np.deg2rad(deg)
    v1 = np.array([1.0, 0.0])
    v2 = np.array([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])
    axT.annotate("", xy=(off+v1[0], v1[1]), xytext=(off, 0),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2))
    axT.annotate("", xy=(off+v2[0], v2[1]), xytext=(off, 0),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.2))
    axT.text(off+0.1, -0.28, f"{deg}°", fontsize=10, color=GREY)
axT.set_xlim(-0.5, 11); axT.set_ylim(-0.5, 1.3)
axT.set_yticks([]); axT.set_xticks([])
axT.set_title("Two vectors, shrinking angle (independent until they coincide)")
axT.set_aspect("equal")

# Bottom: smallest singular value vs angle (a continuous closeness-to-dependence)
fine = np.linspace(90, 1, 200)
smin = []
for deg in fine:
    th = np.deg2rad(deg)
    M = np.column_stack([[1.0, 0.0], [np.cos(th), np.sin(th)]])
    smin.append(np.linalg.svd(M, compute_uv=False).min())
axB = axes[1]
axB.plot(fine, smin, color=POINT)
axB.scatter(angles, [np.linalg.svd(np.column_stack(
    [[1.0,0.0],[np.cos(np.deg2rad(d)), np.sin(np.deg2rad(d))]]),
    compute_uv=False).min() for d in angles], color=ARROW, zorder=3, s=40)
axB.set_xlabel("angle between the vectors (degrees)")
axB.set_ylabel(r"smallest singular value $\sigma_{\min}$")
axB.set_title(r"$\sigma_{\min}\to 0$ as the pair approaches dependence")
axB.invert_xaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
```

The lesson: in applied work the question is rarely "are these *exactly* dependent?" but
"are they *close enough* to dependent to wreck the computation?" That closeness is measured
by the **condition number** and the smallest singular value, the subjects of [GEOM-26] and
[GEOM-20]. The clean binary of @def-independence is the limiting case; the dial is what you
actually live with. Recognizing that near-dependence is as dangerous as exact dependence —
and that `numpy.linalg.matrix_rank` decides the binary by *thresholding* that dial at a
tolerance — is the difference between a textbook understanding and a working one.

## When `matrix_rank` lies to you {#sec-rank-lies}

Because the rank routine thresholds singular values, its answer depends on the threshold,
and on floating-point arithmetic that never represents a number like $10.7639$ exactly.
The next snippet shows the trap: a matrix that is *exactly* rank-deficient in intent can
read as full-rank, and a near-dependent matrix can flip its reported rank as you nudge the
tolerance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rank-tol
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Reported rank is a thresholding decision, not a fact of nature. A matrix whose third column is the first plus $10^{-9}$ times noise is *mathematically* full rank, but its reported rank drops from 3 to 2 as the tolerance crosses the noise scale. Whenever you call `matrix_rank` on real data you are choosing where on this staircase to stand."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
base = rng.normal(size=(20, 2))
# third column = first column + tiny noise: nearly, not exactly, dependent
col3 = base[:, [0]] + 1e-9 * rng.normal(size=(20, 1))
M = np.hstack([base, col3])

tols = np.logspace(-12, -6, 60)
ranks = [np.linalg.matrix_rank(M, tol=t) for t in tols]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
ax.step(tols, ranks, where="post", color=POINT)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("rank tolerance"); ax.set_ylabel("reported rank")
ax.set_yticks([1, 2, 3])
ax.set_title("The same matrix has different 'rank' at different tolerances")
plt.tight_layout()
print("reported rank at tol=1e-12:", np.linalg.matrix_rank(M, tol=1e-12))
print("reported rank at tol=1e-7 :", np.linalg.matrix_rank(M, tol=1e-7))
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never treat a computed rank as ground truth about independence.** `numpy.linalg.matrix_rank`
counts singular values above a tolerance; change the tolerance and the answer can change.
Exactly-dependent columns can read as independent (rounding lifts a zero singular value off
zero), and near-dependent columns are *reported* independent while being numerically
useless. The honest tools are the singular values themselves ([GEOM-20]) and the condition
number ([GEOM-26]) — a *spectrum* of closeness-to-dependence — not a single integer. The
integer is a convenience that has discarded exactly the borderline information you most
need.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the linear-independence framework the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"*
The honest summary:

- **Use it** to reason about whether features carry distinct information, to find and remove
  exact redundancies (duplicate units, dummy-variable traps, sum-to-one constraints), and as
  the foundation for *every* downstream notion of "how many real directions are here" — rank
  ([GEOM-12]), the four subspaces, the dimension of a solution set.
- **Do not lean on the bare binary** when data are noisy or measured: real features are
  almost never *exactly* dependent, so "independent / dependent" is too crude. Reach instead
  for the continuous measures — singular values, condition number, variance inflation factors
  in regression — that quantify *how close* to dependence you are.
- **And remember the boundary:** linear independence sees only *linear* redundancy. Two
  features can be perfectly predictable from each other through a *nonlinear* relationship
  (say $y = x^2$) and still be linearly independent — the framework will call them
  independent because no straight-line combination collapses them. Independence is a
  statement about linear structure only.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-2 *Span and Subspaces*]**, which built the set of places a handful
of vectors can reach and flagged that the count of vectors need not match the size of that
set. This article closed that gap: **independence** says which vectors pull their weight,
a **basis** is the leanest full description, and **dimension** is the forced count of
independent directions — the first genuinely *invariant* number the track has produced.
With Movement I (vectors, combinations, span, independence) complete, the trunk forks:

- **[GEOM-4 *The Dot Product*]** and **[GEOM-5 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*]** —
  among all bases, the *orthonormal* ones (mutually perpendicular, unit length) are the
  nicest, because then the coordinates of @thm-unique-coords are just dot products. This
  article said *a* basis exists and is efficient; GEOM-5 builds the *best* kind.
- **[GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four Fundamental Subspaces*]** — the rank we computed in the
  worked examples is *defined* as the dimension of the column space, and the rank–nullity
  theorem is dimension-counting (@lem-steinitz, @thm-invariance) made into the master
  bookkeeping identity for a matrix.
- **[GEOM-14 *Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors*]** — eigenvectors, when there are enough of
  them, form a basis (@def-basis) chosen so a matrix acts by simple scaling along each
  basis direction; whether such a basis *exists* is exactly an independence question.
- **Downstream, into [REGR-7]:** exact linear dependence among predictors is perfect
  multicollinearity (Example 1, Example 2); the near-dependence dial of @sec-near-dependence
  is the practical version that ridge regression and the condition number tame.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary source.** Hermann Grassmann, *Die lineale Ausdehnungslehre* (1844, revised
  1862) — the first treatment of dimension as the number of independent quantities, decades
  ahead of its time and famously hard to read; the modern definitions in this article are
  its descendants, cleaned up by the twentieth-century axiomatization of vector spaces.
- **The counting lemma.** @lem-steinitz is the *Steinitz exchange lemma* (Ernst Steinitz,
  1913), the result from which the invariance of dimension — and most of finite-dimensional
  linear algebra's counting — flows. Worth knowing by name.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do we actually compute a basis
  and rank reliably?* — via QR or the SVD, not naive elimination, for the numerical reasons
  of @sec-rank-lies; built in [GEOM-7] and [GEOM-20]. *What is the cleanest possible basis?*
  — an orthonormal one, [GEOM-5]. *How does dimension partition a matrix's input and output
  space?* — the four fundamental subspaces and rank–nullity, [GEOM-12]. *And the question
  underneath dimension reduction:* given data that only *nearly* lives in a low-dimensional
  flat, what is its *effective* dimension? — the SVD's singular values answer it ([GEOM-20]),
  and Unsupervised Learning's PCA turns that answer into a statistical method.
